- How Airbus Surpassed Boeing To Become The World’s Top Plane Maker: Kind: captions Language: en Airbus and Boeing have been competing head to head for decades as the world's largest commercial airplane makers. The European based Airbus entered the market 56 years after its rival, and today, 76% of the world's fleet is made up of Airbus and Boeing planes. But over the last few years, Airbus has edged out Boeing for market dominance. Airbus is clearly in the lead and they have a good lead. And if you just want to measure that, it's by backlog of the airplanes. Airbus has made a steady profit since the pandemic, while Boeing hasn't made a profit since 2018. Airbus total backlog, which are planes that have been ordered but not yet delivered, is 8726, compared to Boeing's 5643, which represents billions of dollars in revenue and is estimated to take almost 13 years to complete. We've gone from back in 1978 where you would hardly ever see an Airbus airplane in the US, to now having well over 50% of the market here in the US. Since 2019, Airbus has delivered more planes annually than Boeing, which has been dealing with crisis after crisis, including two deadly crashes of its best selling 737 Max eight, which grounded that aircraft for nearly two years. It's also been dealing with significant financial losses, manufacturing issues and production limitations. One thing I want to be clear about Airbus was already winning the race before the Max ran into trouble because the A321 in general was winning share. Narrowbodies make up the majority of the commercial aircraft flying today. These planes are very versatile. They can do multiple short trips a day or across oceans and continents. Boeing's 737 was the world's best selling narrowbody until 2019, when Airbus A320 surpassed it in total orders. And depending on how many planes are delivered in 2025, the A320 could take the reigns as the most produced jet family in history. If you look across the board at the backlogs and indeed at and orders. Everything is fine in every category except for the A321, which is absolutely crushing it. Cnbc explores how Airbus came to be the biggest commercial plane manufacturer and whether it can stay on top. The official 737 christening ceremony took place in the new final Assembly building. In the late 60s. Boeing was dominating the commercial aircraft market. It had just debuted the 737 and the world's first ever widebody jet. The massive 747 European aerospace companies took notice. The biggest misconception about Airbus is that it was some sort of instant creation, circa 1969. It really wasn't. It was a merger of a number of European national champion aerospace companies that had been building planes since World War II or World War One, and the only way they could do it was by pooling their resources. Thus, Airbus. As more people took to the skies, airlines looked to operate bigger aircraft. At the time, all widebody planes had 3 or 4 engines. For reference, a widebody typically has two aisles, flies longer distances, and can carry anywhere from 250 to 500 passengers, while a narrow body or single aisle has one single aisle through. The cabin flies shorter distances and can have 100 to 230 seats. Airbus shows up in the 1970s saying, hey, we have a widebody plane, but with just two engines, and airlines sat up and said, whoa, two engines and obviously two engines that can take you the same distance as three are better because your maintenance costs are a lot lower. In 1972, Airbus introduced the first twin engine widebody jetliner, the A300. Despite having only two engines, the A300 could only carry around 250 passengers versus the massive 747, which could be configured to fit over 500. Not a lot of people saw the need for an intermediate sized jet, so production rates were 1 or 2 per month for quite a few years. In 1978, the Deregulation Act was enacted in the US, creating more competition, new airlines and expansion of smaller ones. As airline needs shifted in the 80s, the demand for cost effective short haul aircraft grew. They began to see the need for a new generation single aisle basically centered around 150 seats. Airbus had not yet made a profit, and the company needed a plane in its fleet to compete with Boeing's popular narrowbody, the 737, which had already been around since 1967. Airbus launched the A320 single aisle program in 1984. The workhorse of the Airbus commercial jet family is the A320. The A320 was one of the few commercial aircraft designed to compete against Boeing's 737 powerhouse, and has succeeded where others have failed. Its fuselage is a little bit wider than the 737, and some airlines have taken advantage of that to put in slightly wider seats. The A320 debuted with a lot of new advanced technology. It was one of the first commercial jets that used a sidestick versus yoke controls. This is still a key difference between Airbus and Boeing planes today. It also introduced fly by wire controls, meaning when you pull back on the Sidestick, it sends electronic signals to a computer to move the aircraft versus a physical cable. One could argue from a flight controls point of view, the A320 is a generation ahead of the 737. When it first came into the market, it was controversial. Fly by wire when it first came out into the market was something that was really just used in military airplanes. The Jets debut, however, ended in a crash during the first demonstration flight at an airshow in 1988. When the A320 was first introduced, it suffered a series of accidents, including some fatal accidents that killed crew and passengers Seizures, and that really stalled the public acceptance of the plane. It took a while there, too, to get traction, but when it did, well, um, it succeeded far beyond expectations. A320 just came into the world 20 years later and has that advantage in terms of just fundamental design differences to the airframe. By the 1990s, the A320 success would start to put pressure on its US competitors. Airbus, which was backed by European governments, made its first operating profits. Once Airbus was more market proven as an aircraft builder and US airlines started to see the performance results of the Airbus aircraft and other airlines fleets, they became more open to buying Airbus planes, and it was really the A320 that helped Airbus crack the door open into the US market and many other parts of the world. Basically, Boeing through all of this had 50% of the market. So what you basically had was Douglas, Lockheed and Airbus duking it out for the other half. Lockheed exited sometime in the mid 80s and then everyone. New McDonnell Douglas matter of time. In 1997, McDonnell Douglas merged with Boeing, forming the duopoly we know today. If you think about the evolution of Airbus and Airbus really started in the narrowbody market with no market share, edged out Douglas, and then continued to get share. The Boeing 737 family and the Airbus A320 family are the company's best selling planes and have been a crucial segment of competition. The list price for one A321neo is around $129 million. Competition was head to head when it came to widebodies, too. In the 90s, more fuel efficient twin engine widebodies were introduced. Airbus debuted the A330 and A340, while Boeing introduced its 777, the A330. They kept refining it at Airbus, it did pretty well, and then Boeing attacked with the 787 Dreamliner, and despite all the flaws baked into that development program, it did better than the A330 in the long run. Then, from 2003 to 2005, Airbus took more orders and delivered more planes than Boeing. That 50 over 50 formula kept persisting, and what Boeing was doing was effectively winning on profitability rather than market share. They were willing to say, all right, we're going to be 50%, but our margins are going to be 9%. Yours are going to be four or less. It was a formula that worked for them until, of course, the problems with the max. Despite declining sales for four engine jets, Airbus spent billions creating the biggest double decker quad engine commercial aircraft in the world and possibly the most identifiable Airbus plane, the A380. It was in service from 2007 until it ended production in 2021. The A380 is an engineering marvel. Unfortunately, in a way, the A380 success was eclipsed by the success of Airbus's smaller aircraft, namely the A330 family and more recently, the A350, which are twin engine aircraft that can hold 250 to 400, 450 or so passengers and operate ultra long range routes. And you looked at Airbus and you thought, wow, you're going to be tied for number one. You might even be number one. The only thing you could do to screw that up is launch a superjumbo for a market that's going away super fast. And that's exactly what they did. It cost them a decade. Today, Boeing still dominates the widebody market with nearly 56% of the global fleet. These aircraft are used for long haul travel like international flights. Boeing currently has a backlog of over 1500 widebody planes. The company has had multiple manufacturing issues with its 787 Dreamliner and the 777X, which has yet to be certified, is years behind schedule. In 2012, Airbus announced it would begin manufacturing in mobile, Alabama. You go back to 1978, and Airbus had a small office here in the US with about a dozen people working there. Roll forward to 2025, and we have a very large manufacturing facility that manufactures airplanes in mobile, Alabama. We build helicopters in Mississippi, we build satellites in Florida, and actually our presence in over 40 locations across the Today, Airbus biggest customer is Delta Air Lines. It has slightly more Airbus planes in its fleet than Airbus has had a different manufacturing strategy than Boeing. Theirs has been more regional. So they have a final assembly plant, a foul, as they call them in Asia, one in North America, one in Europe, where Boeing's final assembly has been highly concentrated in in North America. China was almost exclusively Boeing customer for many, many years by having a final assembly line in China. I think Airbus would argue that that helped them get some share in China. Airbus has been manufacturing in the US for nearly ten years. It's about to open a third production line, two of which are for the A320 family and the other for its smaller 220 jets it acquired from Bombardier in 2020. In 2010, Airbus announced a new variation of its best selling narrowbody called the A320neo, short for New Engine option, which promised a 15% less fuel burn than the previous generation. In 2011, Airbus had a record number of orders for 1419 aircraft, more than double than the year before. That same year, American Airlines, which had been strictly a Boeing customer, put in an order for hundreds of these new planes. And in 2019, the A320 family surpassed the 737 in total orders. Originally, the business case was for around 600 airplanes and now we are up to 19,000. Boeing's response to the more fuel efficient A320 was the re-engined 737 Max that debuted in 2017, but over the next few years, the manufacturer faced a series of challenges. In 2018 and 2019, two 737 Max eight crashed and killed 346 people. It was later found a flight control system known as MCAS had malfunctioned on both flights. The planes were grounded for several months and production rates were reduced. Then the pandemic brought air travel to a halt, and the company had a record loss of $12 billion in 2020. Airbus saw losses of over 1 billion in both 2019 and 2020, but returned to profitability in 2021. But Airbus has consecutively had more annual orders and deliveries since 2019, making it the world's largest planemaker. Then in 2024, a door plug flew off of Boeing 737 Max nine plane, leading to more production delays of its best selling jet and an annual loss of $11.8 billion in 2024. Airbus has profited from the challenges that Boeing has faced. Some airlines decided to place orders with Airbus for aircraft rather than And if you look at Airbus's backlog today, it's dominated by A321. Airlines looking to cut down on fuel costs have been turning to these more efficient, longer range narrowbodies for longer flights like JFK to LA or the East Coast to Europe. Of our single aisle order book or backlog, as we call it, 72% of the Airbus A321. And that's opening up routes and markets that they couldn't fly directly before. The economics with single aisles were so much better than the economics with widebodies. And the Airbus A321neo wasn't perfect, but it was the biggest and best single aisle you could get, and leading Airbus to effectively a 60 or 65% market share from the standpoint of both deliveries and orders and backlog. It's become the hinge factor between the two. Boeing doesn't really have a comparable airplane. 737 Max nine isn't quite as big. The ten is big, but it doesn't quite have the range. - Why Hydrogen-Powered Planes Might Be Inevitable: Kind: captions Language: en It seems like everything is going green these days but there's one mode of transportation that seems to be clinging to the traditional, fuel-loving ways. And while commercial aviation has made strides through smarter design and cleaner engines, many of those gains have been nullified by more air traffic. The challenge is enormous. This is a large industry. It will take decades to convert. The good news is that we will have a solution as early as three years out that people can get into and start moving zero emission. And it turns out that the solution could be all around us. Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe. And so if you had to use something, it would be great. The only product of the chemical reaction between hydrogen and oxygen is water. We looked at the fundamentals of what it would require to take an aircraft up in the air of significant size, over significant distance, commercially relevant. You get your hydrogen fuel cell being the best approach from the cost of fuel, efficiency of utilization of the fuel and the mitigation of the climate effects. Serial cleantech entrepreneur Val Miftakhov started ZeroAvia following his previous success in the EV charging industry. So the beauty about hydrogen in general is that the energy density of hydrogen as fuel is actually three times better than jet fuel. So you can see any size of aircraft going for any distance that jet fuel aircraft can go over time. It will just take significant amount of time to get the industry over, but this technology can scale to all sizes of aircraft that we use in commercial service. ZeroAvia uses a hydrogen fuel cell to produce electricity to turn a propeller. Unlike a traditional engine which uses combustion to create energy, a fuel cell generates electricity through an electrochemical reaction. In this case, hydrogen and oxygen are combined to generate electricity, heat and water. ZeroAvia has flown a six-seater aircraft on a hydrogen fuel cell; a world first. Now they're aiming bigger, at 20 seats. That's technically commercial. One side of the aircraft, the left side, will replace the engine with our power plants. We still got a normal engine on the right side. And part of this is, in aviation you want to ramp up risk profile in meaningful steps. So anything happens, we have the second engine. But even in the first flight test campaign, what we're planning to do is to demonstrate operation of this aircraft purely on zero emission power on the left side engine. Once we take off, we're able to switch over to completely zero emission power. Hydrogen is used throughout all sections of a flight, that maximizes the efficiency of the entire operation that reduces the weight, provides for best sort of mission capabilities, payload and range. Building power plants and fuel cells is one thing, creating a whole new infrastructure for supplying hydrogen is something entirely different. In automotive a big part of the reason why hydrogen did not take off is because the fueling infrastructure needs to be so distributed. For let's say, United States, you have a hundred thousand fueling stations. Compare that with aviation where 95% or more traffic in the United States is concentrated in 100 locations. So that's a three order magnitude difference which makes build out of the infrastructure much simpler. It's much larger, much more concentrated stations, but they're much fewer in quantity. Calling it simpler might be underselling the challenge of distributing hydrogen, which unlike other fuels that can be easily transported in liquid form, is usually found instead in a gaseous state. How do you get the hydrogen from point A to point B? We don't have pipelines from moving hydrogen around. We don't have all the specialized trucks to move them around and so we got thinking, we need a solution that can be a low capital expenditure solution and that's the genesis of the company. John-Paul Clarke is the co-founder and Chief Innovation Officer of Universal Hydrogen, a startup that has designed a modular tank system for the domestic turbo prop market. Like ZeroAvia, it allows for retrofitting planes already in use. Each module has two capsules and so what we do is load it into the aircraft as if it was cargo, strap it down, connect it to the aircraft, close the loading door, and that would be it. When you get to your destination, you'd basically unload it, put it back in a truck, send it back to the production site to get refills. We needed to basically come up with something that could both fit in containers and also fit in the aircraft, not require increases in the maximum takeoff weight of the aircraft. This compromise is one of the biggest hurdles that may prevent widespread adoption of hydrogen. Obviously, you're gonna have to take out some seats because the energy density of hydrogen is less than jet fuel and you can't store it in the wings practically, so you're gonna have to take away some space in the fuselage. In the ultra-fine margins of aviation, removing 10 to 20% of your seats is a tough ask, but that hasn't deterred startups like ZeroAvia or Universal Hydrogen. The maintenance cost of a fuel cell motor system goes down and it goes down significantly, because motors and fuel cells have much fewer moving parts than a gas turbine engine. And so the wear and tear is much lower and therefore the time between overhaul is longer. And so when you put all that together, our numbers indicate that the CASM, Cost per Available Seat Mile, actually goes down slightly or is at the worst equivalent to what you have now. So what you'll have is a smaller cabin or smaller number of seats. However, the cost for each of those seats to operate it is the same or better with the hydrogen. Like ZeroAvia, Universal Hydrogen is also working on engines, successfully testing their two megawatt iron bird that will allow them to retrofit planes carrying up to 55 passengers. ZeroAvia is aiming for their first commercial hydrogen electric flight between London and Rotterdam with their 19-seater by around 2024. But much like the range anxiety that has plagued some battery-powered electric vehicles on land, hydrogen fuel cells are also fairly limited. At this point, they still don't have the capacity to power a common 100-passenger jet. Beyond fuel cells, however, there is another hope for our lightest, most abundant element: Burning it. The first eureka moment, I suppose, is when we saw steam in the exhaust because we are thinking to ourselves, "Huh? We're burning something here when you're producing absolutely no CO2." An expert in gas turbine combustion, professor Bobby Sethi leads research at Cranfield University in the U.K. Built on a former RAF base, the college runs multiple aeronautical programs. But what Bobby is focused on is burning hydrogen, as cleanly as possible. So what we are trying to demonstrate in this rig is how we can conceive some hydrogen combustion technologies that can be integrated in the next generation aircraft engines, which will deliver not only zero CO2 emissions, but also ultra-low NOx emissions. NOx or nitrogen oxides are a significant source of air pollution globally. They're the dirty particulates that cause smog in cities, usually spat out by diesel cars, scooters and buses. Hydrogen is characterized by much wider flammability limits, which means we can go to much leaner combustion. And as a result, we can burn at much lower flame temperatures and that's better for reducing NOx emissions. Going leaner means burning fuel with an excess of air in the engine. Using something like gasoline, lean burn emits far fewer hydrocarbons. Doing it with hydrogen also delivers cleaner emissions. While NOx is a pollutant, hydrogen combustion produces up to 90% less nitrogen oxide than kerosene. So while burning hydrogen isn't technically as clean as using it inside a fuel cell, it's still a huge improvement over jet fuel. Unfortunately, like any radically new idea, there are other potential byproducts of the process that aren't fully yet understood. NOx is clearly one of the main emissions we need to consider, but the aviation community is also asking themselves and the community in general, what about all the water vapor emissions? And some studies have shown, albeit there's still a large degree of uncertainty about it, that contrails and cirrus clouds that may be induced from contrails could contribute to global warming about four times the amount than CO2 does. We know that if we are going to be burning hydrogen, we are going to produce much larger amount of water vapor emissions. And if we are going to be producing a much larger amount of water vapor emissions, then the propensity for contrail formation is also going to increase. In spite of all these challenges, hydrogen is being taken seriously by the broader industry. Companies like Universal Hydrogen and ZeroAvia are gaining the attention of investors looking for viable and eventually profitable solutions. Industry giant Airbus wants to introduce a hydrogen-powered passenger aircraft by 2035. Recently announcing plans to retrofit a gas-guzzling Superjumbo with a hydrogen-burning engine. The modified aircraft will add a fifth engine, adapted for hydrogen, and will be mounted on the rear fuselage. The A380 is the largest passenger aircraft in existence, so it offers plenty of room to store the 400 kilograms of hydrogen. The company's designs for a blended wing concept to store extra hydrogen also offer a glimpse of our flying future. Apart from the complex engineering challenges faced by airplane manufacturers, there are also difficulties in production of hydrogen itself. Nowadays, most of the hydrogen used in fuel is derived by splitting it off from molecules of natural gas. But that requires a good deal of energy and also produces carbon dioxide. To make green hydrogen, the electricity used to run the electrolyzer must come from a renewable resource, which is currently a lot more expensive. As with any new technology however, initial costs are daunting, but time may be the best remedy here. If you look at economics of hydrogen fuel production, for example, versus fossil fuels, for instance, the cost of hydrogen is all based on capital expense and very little operating expense. So it's sort of similar to solar power. You put solar panels out there, and they produce power for 20-25 years. The operating expense is relatively low. What that means is that as scale grows, as we've seen with the solar panels, the cost of output drops dramatically. So the first phase is not about the most efficient aircraft. It's not about the aircraft that will deliver the lowest NOx emissions. It's about demonstrating that we can carry hydrogen safely on board, we can burn it safely on board, and can be used to fly a passenger aircraft. The key thing is that we don't try to put everything in the first generation or we're just gonna delay the entry into service. So to try and keep it as simple as possible. If there are some questions on the margin, they're all in the sort of business model and market adoption realm. It is very hard to create a technological argument or impossible to create a technological argument that says, "Well, it's not gonna work." It is going to work.

  3. @rsailor971: For everyone confused by the "hydrogen is 3 times as energy dense as jet fuel" comment, the correct statement is: hydrogen has 3 times the energy per unit of mass as jet fuel. This is known as specific energy, not "energy density". It is sometimes referred to as "gravimetric energy density".

For everyone confused about the need for heavy, high-pressure hydrogen gas tanks: Only liquid hydrogen is being talked about in this video. The tanks are only pressurized to a few atmospheres. They are not heavy. 

For everyone confused about the need for large, heavy liquid hydrogen tanks: The combined weight of the tanks and the liquid hydrogen needed is about 1/2 that of jet fuel tanks and fuel. 

For everyone who is confused about the large volume of liquid hydrogen tanks: generally, you can't fit these into existing airliners, and no one is planning to. Designing new aircraft that use liquid hydrogen is not a challenge. (Except for the "challenged".)

For those who know that most hydrogen is currently produced from natural gas, that's why no one is planning to use natural gas to produce green hydrogen. (Duh.) 

For everyone concerned about the safety of transporting liquid hydrogen, it has been safely transported in bulk on North American highways for close to 60 years now.

Potentially it would be a lot less expensive than a fighter jet. The goal of these systems is to be mass-producible. And we tried to eliminate really every bottleneck we could find around what makes an aircraft hard to produce. Uh so really simple things that we've been able to do are design the landing gear. For example, instead of using very exquisite big aircraft landing gear, which are hard to produce, really limited supply, we designed it so it can be built in any machine shop in America, instead of using a military engine, we've been able to use a commercial business jet engine that are mass-produced and readily available. And we've designed nearly every part of this that could be made in hundreds of different places within the US from lots of different suppliers. Fury is scheduled to take its first test flight this summer. The Air Force hopes to have CCAs fully operational before the end of this decade. And right now, we're proving out the aircraft and getting through all the engineering, but we're also working through how will these things be used in combat. We're working through simulations and ways these can actually be employed. This is a big deal beyond just making an airplane that flies. It's an entirely new way of fighting.

Do we cut corners and fly around in a less capable, less safe Air Force One?" Here in the US, the rise of private flying is closely tied with the history of Air Force One. Franklin Roosevelt became the first president to fly in a private purpose-built plane in 1945 when he traveled to Crimea during World War II. Roosevelt's plane had a conference room, a special wheelchair lift, a bulletproof window, and a refrigerator, the epitome of luxury at the time. In 1953, President Dwight Eisenhower became the first to officially call his plane the Air Force One, when he upgraded to a jet, a Boeing 707. 10 years later, in 1963, a Swiss American aviation company, Learjet, introduced the first private aircraft with seats for six passengers and two pilots. Its price tag started at about $650,000. In the late '7s, Boeing began converting its popular 747 model for private luxury travel. Over the next two decades, charter services emerged, allowing people to easily explore and book private trips, ushering in a new era of flying. [Applause] Qatar has been building its own aviation empire since 1993 while using planes as a diplomacy tool. In 2000, the country sent a Boeing 747 to Iraq as an expression of solidarity. In 2013, when Turkish and Lebanese hostages were released from Syria, Qatar executive quickly offered jets to escort them home. And in 2018, Qatar's Amir gifted a Boeing 747 to Turkish President Arduan, citing his trust in Turkey. I don't think there's any question that they regard aviation as a kind of an integrated well fabric if you will, economics, diplomacy, uh, military power, so hard and soft power. They there's no way they could be spending this monster amount of money on jets without having a sense of what it all means. >> Becoming an aviation powerhouse has also helped Qatar secure its position in the Middle East. When tensions escalated between Qatar and its neighbors in 2017 over its ties to Iran, Egypt, the UAE, Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia imposed a massive blockade. They denied the tiny kingdom access to their airspace and seas and closed its only land border. Qatar relied on imports for survival and the country was facing a crisis. It quickly turned to its aviation superiority to flex some muscle. The airline flew thousands of dairy cows on Qatar Airways cargo planes from Germany, the Netherlands, the US, and Australia to Doha, where a new farm was waiting to help give the country more control over its food supply. >> Aviation was their lifeline. The Qatar Investment Authority, the country's $500 billion sovereign wealth fund, has bought shares in other airlines. In 2020, Qatar secured its 25% stake in IA, which operates British Airways, Iberia, and Heirlingus among others. And back in 2012, the country bought a 20% stake in London's Heathro airport. It also owns part of two African airlines, Airlink and Rwanda Air. Qatar's ties to the United States also run deep. Qatar Airways has an order for 210 aircraft. And Qatar Executive flies 22 private jets made by another American manufacturer, Gulfream, with a few more scheduled for delivery next year. The private jet business is also growing worldwide. Today, there are about 23,000 private jets operating around the world, and about 65% of those are based in the US. That's up from 10,000 jets operating globally in 2000. Analysts say the global business jet market could reach nearly $70 billion by 2032. But owning a private jet isn't the only option. Others book and share chartered planes, or they use jet card programs that are like a prepaid card for flight hours. How you make sure that you have enough availability, but at the same time, we can't have too much waste. >> To avoid empty seats, the company also sells individual chartered flights outside the membership program. And they're looking for ways to reduce their carbon footprint. Studies have found that private flights are up to 14 times more polluting per passenger than flying commercial as a carbon footprint is spread across fewer people. One study calculated that 2 hours of a private flight emits more carbon dioxide than an average person creates in a year. ROMAINE: AS YOU DEPLOY IT INTERNALLY, I ASSUME YOU'RE USING THE SAME LINES TO DECIDE WHAT TO INVEST IN. TONY: WE HAVE 12 PEOPLE INTERNALLY THAT WE HAVE HIRED FROM EITHER CONSULTING FIRMS OR TECH SHOPS THAT ARE HELPING THE ENTIRE ORGANIZATION, WHETHER INVESTMENTS AND CAPITAL FORMATION, FINANCE, OPERATIONS, CLIENT SERVICE, UPSCALE, USING THE TECHNOLOGY. THEY ARE EMBEDDED WITH OUR TECHNOLOGY TEAM THAT LOOKS AT INVESTMENTS. WE ARE NOT GOING TO SEE A NEW ADVANCEMENT IN TECH, AI AND SAY HERE IS A SOFTWARE COMPANY THAT WE MIGHT HAVE INVESTED IN OR MADE A MISTAKE IN, WE NEED TO GET OUT OF. WE HAVE BEEN SEEING THAT PLAY OUT OVER THE PAST COUPLE OF MONTHS. TONY: LENDERS TOOK SOLACE IN THE FACT THAT YOU WERE A LAOAN TO VALUE. YOU WOULD TAKE COMFORT IN THE FACT OF SOMEBODY ELSE IS BUYING A BUSINESS AND YOU ARE LENDING $.40 ON THE DOLLAR RELATIVE TO THE MARKET. AT THE END OF THE DAY, $.40, 40% LOAN TO VALUE, YOU MIGHT BE EIGHT TO 10 TIMES LEVERED IN THAT BUSINESS. ALL OF CASH FLOW GOES TO SERVICE THE DEBT. YOU CANNOT INVEST IN THE FUTURE OF THE BUSINESS. THOSE SOFTWARE COMPANIES WILL NEED TO EVOLVE INTO AN ORCHESTRATION LAYER ON TOP OF AI IN ORDER TO EVOLVE AND SURVIVE. IF YOU HAVE TOO MUCH DEBT, YOU CANNOT INVEST AND PIVOT YOUR BUSINESS. IT FEELS LIKE THAT HAS BLOOMED AS A NEW AS A CLASS OF ITS OWN. TONY: WE FINANCE SOME DATA CENTER BUILDS, WE HAVE FINANCED COLOSSUS, XAI'S DATA CENTER IN MEMPHIS, TENNESSEE. THEY HAVE A COUPLE OF TURBINES THAT WE HAVE FINANCED, ABOUT $600 MILLION WORTH OF DEBT IN THOSE. THAT AMORTIZE IS DOWN IN CASH FLOW. WE HAVE LENT TO THE BUILT IN THE INFRASTRUCTURE BUILDOUT. DATA CENTERS ARE HERE TO STAY. IT WILL BE A POLITICAL ISSUE IN THE NEXT THREE TO SIX MONTHS. YOU SAW WHAT HAPPENED AT SAM ALTMAN'S HOUSE, HE WAS ATTACKED, A MOLOTOV COCKTAIL THAT WAS THROWN. IT IS NOT GOING TO GO AWAY. YOU CAN GET MORE DONE WITH LITTLE EFFORT. YOU CAN HAVE BETTER CONVERSATIONS WITH YOUR COLLEAGUES. YOU CAN MAKE MORE INFORMED INVESTMENT DECISIONS. THAT IS REALLY WHAT WE ARE FOCUSED ON DOING. I DO THINK, BY AND LARGE, THE FUTURE OF OFFERING A CERTAIN AMOUNT OF LIQUIDITY PER QUARTER IS THE RIGHT THING TO DO FOR PRODUCTS LIKE THIS. IT PROTECTS THE EXISTING INVESTORS. THE WAY THE INDUSTRY HAS HANDLED THIS HAS GONE VERY ORDERLY. I DON'T HAVE AN ISSUE WITH THE FUTURE, PROBABLY THAT WE SHOULD JUST USE LESS LINGO. BUT THE PUBLIC MARKETS HAVE BEEN ON AN INCREDIBLE RUN. 15 YEARS OF 10% THAT RETURN FOR THAT BORING 60/40 PORTFOLIO. IT IS NOT ALWAYS WHAT HAS HAPPENED IN THE PAST. PHENOMENAL RUN IN EQUITY MARKETS, WHO KNOWS WHAT HAPPENS IN THE SECOND HALF OF THE YEAR, BUT WE ARE ON PACE FOR ANOTHER DOUBLE-DIGIT RETURN ON THE S&P 500. BUT A SKILLED MANAGER SHOULD BE ABLE TO USE THAT YOU LIQUIDITY PREMIUM TO THEIR ADVANTAGE. THAT IS WHERE WE ARE FOCUSED. WE SEE THOSE TYPES OF OPPORTUNITIES IN PRIVATE EQUITY. price until your customers are complaining but still paying is the right way to go all right let's talk about the thing that most Founders get wrong I've seen as many times but there are a few things that most wanted to get wrong and this is one of them working backwards from your goal with your sales funnel in order to work your way backwards from a goal of say two signed customers you have to understand that each step in the sales funnel is going to have a drop-off when you haven't started sales yet you don't know what these draft off percentages are maybe you haven't done a lot of sales before you don't know what you're not good at maybe you're really good at sending emails but really bad at closing or vice versa you don't know that so as you're sending these outbound emails you need Before you let an assistant touch real workflows, it needs to prove 3 things, in my opinion. So, first thing is, of course, accuracy. Your AI should be treated like an intern on day one. So you're checking for factual accuracy. Alignment with your policies, correct interpretation of rules, appropriate tone. And then a simple test is the silent work audit. You give the assistant task. But you compare its results to what a human expert would do. And if it can't match your standard consistently, it's not ready. Second is privacy considerations. One that tends to get missed, um, quite often. And my professional opinion, privacy should not be optional. Privacy is protection for your business, your idea, and yourself. So this means knowing what data can and cannot be exposed utilizing your co-pilot. It means avoiding personally identifiable information unless your tools are compliant. Or understanding how your platform stores and processes data, just having a stronger understanding of it. Or ensuring that your assistant never reveals internal information out of context. So, if the data would require a HIPAA-level safeguard with a human involved, then your AI has to follow that same human guardrail. You don't get to bypass that guardrail. And then third to that is ethical guardrails. A reliable AI assistant knows what not to act. So you must explicitly teach your AI co-pilot to never guess. Right? You must teach it to ask for clarification when uncertain, and you should teach it how to escalate anything outside of its authority. AI becomes dangerous only when it improvises. Um, I'm going to leave it very broad there to allow you to explain what that is. It sounds very Terminator. Um, but obviously this is what you do at Safe. Um, safe is spelled S A I F for anyone who is uh going to look at this up after this episode. Um, but how did you meet Matt and how did you know he was the one that was going to be your partner moving forward, you know, outside of the military? >> Yeah, it's it's it's a great story actually how we met. So, so my last job in in the Air Force was in something called the uh rapid capabilities office. Uh and that is it was set up um to as it kind of says on the tin rapidly deliver new capability to the air force but looking into sort of frontier technologies like how can we solve a problem maybe do it in 6 months to a year rather than 3 to 5 years and bring things in that are very low technology readiness level and for those that don't know what that means it's actually a NASA scale that tech adopted uh so one to nine nine being your BMW 3 series you know it just works one being something in a lab that's never been tested uh and so we bring it in around the three tier three and try and kick it over the fence at seven very rapidly where a frontline user could then develop it all the way uh forward from there. uh and and I was running uh a collaborative autonomy program that was looking at sort of medium-siz drones uh for a variety of use cases and one of the big problems with that is Terminator as you said there you know Skynet for those that that know Terminator we're trying to avoid that right so as as we move away from traditional autonomy that's a bit like command and conquer for those that remember that you know click on way points something follows it and it does a scripted behavior somewhere to this use of physical or embodied AI at the edge so that system can freely think and be nondetermed istic on goal-based uh what we call goal based autonomy and that's really efficient and it allows those systems to collaborate together and work out the best way to to you know carry out a mission and maybe do other things along the way without the need for communications in data links which is really important in today's new world where it's very easy to jam uh communications and you may not be able to control that then as a human user right so we were looking at at how how could we do that in the future how could we do it today with today's traditional autonomy uh and there was nothing on the market that actually would provide this assurance to allow this to be used as a frontline capability in the way that we might wish it to be. Uh and so I actually met Matt uh when we contracted his company to go and have a look into academia and science and have a look at what's out there like what could we do quickly uh and potentially quite cost effectively to to enable someone like the Military Aviation Authority to allow these uh systems to operate potentially in you know uh other people's sovereign airspace which is really important because you don't want them doing the things you don't want them to do. Uh and so that that's how we met. When I then left defense, um I I worked very briefly at a company called Round Technologies, who for those that don't know again, it's a very rapidly scaling defense tech uh systems integrator based in Bristol. I went to run their air portfolio. And Matt and I worked on the autonomy team there. As with all these things, we got talking. We realized like there was a huge opportunity here, not just for defense, not just in drones, but actually in anything that moves. So anything that's going to use uh high levels of autonomy uh today or in the future, it needs an assurance layer. It needs a trust layer that regulators and people can believe in uh to allow these systems to just do their thing. And we believe that that can happen in your everyday life all around you will walk around the streets and they'll just be autonomous systems getting about their business. >> Give it give us an example of that. You know, we're talking about autonomous systems. Um and now it depends on the level of understanding the audience has of the this type of product. Uh I very much envision uh maybe a more commercial aspect. So things like >> Amazon delivering my my my product in the sky and coming round and you know we've got Whimo out in the states doing uh autonomous vehicles driving around the streets as your sort of pseudo Uber. Um, and you have I was actually in uh Eastern Europe uh recently and I I saw sort of a an autonomous vehicle on the on the street uh literally just sort of 1 meter by by half a meter just going down the street and finding its way to deliver I'm think groceries. I have absolutely no idea what it was doing but I took a video. It was quite quite entertaining. It's something I'd never seen in the UK. So that's maybe the more commercial aspect. I know that predominantly right now you're you're focusing on more defense um and and other use cases. Could you maybe give us a couple of examples of both of those sides? Maybe what you're working on right now, but then also maybe where let's say the everyday consumer might see it in the future. >> Yeah, absolutely. So I think what's actually quite important to break down here as well is what we mean by autonomy. Um because actually autonomy means different things to different people. So artificial intelligence and autonomy are two different things. So, uh, physical AI, embodied AI, that's where you take, um, a an artificial intelligence model that is trained to operate a vehicle, uh, so a bit like a human and place that onboard a system at the edge. So, on board that vehicle and allow it to have control of that system and like you or I non-deterministically go about piloting, driving that system to a goal, which could be a drop off, a delivery, a task. Now, that's very different to autonomy or autonomous behaviors which might be scripted. They're deterministic. So, go here, follow this route, don't deviate from it. When you get here, do X or Y, which is a predetermined scripted behavior. Drop off a package and you'll do it like this, and then you'll carry on here and what have you. So, that's kind of what I would call autonomy, right? Now, both of those have their own issues because deterministic autonomy actually exhibits nondeterministic behaviors when software goes wrong. So, they both have their issues, right? Uh and so what you see now with companies um like MANA uh who do drone deliveries in Dublin and some places in the US is more of that deterministic autonomy. So it's really waypoint following. Follow a flight path, follow a corridor, go to X, lower your parcel down. Um so so that's a good example of an autonomous system. What you don't see much of yet is the physical AI at the edge controlling a system. So if you've seen any of the NVIDIA videos with robotic dogs running around dancing, doing doing things, you've seen some out of China probably. Now that's physical embodied AI at the edge. So allowing those those robots to actually go out and just do something, tell it to dance and it and it will and it will learn as it's doing it too. And you can take the data and you can retrain those models and make it better at what it does. So that's the distinct difference between the two when it comes to use cases today. So commercially, drone deliveries are the thing that you hear probably most in the media. people talk about it's happening in London with wing flying medical supplies between two hospitals was very very prescriptive straight line kind of stuff if you want to start uh doing that at scale in my opinion and I think the way that a lot of the industry is going outside of the programs that are making money from the the way that it works now you have to get rid of all the infrastructure so having cameras with computer vision every 500 meters all over a city to make sure the airspace is clear is not commercially viable you know it just it's going to cost too much having beacons everywhere for things to follow just doesn't work. What you need to do is take all of that and put it on board as a platform itself cheaply. And that allows the system to go and fly beyond visual line of sight and be trusted to some form of standard. So if you put a trust layer on that that's built to uh say mand aviation certification levels, but you can do that cheaply and guarantee the performance of that platform that will be bound in its behavior, bound in its its geographical locations, but allowed to freeink within that bubble. Now you can send off you know 10020 of those systems uh controlled by AI you know AI uh deciding who goes where how the package deliveries work and then maybe have three or four people in a control room that monitor that whole network and people find that a little bit scary because then what happens to all the drone operators because generally now it's one operator per system maybe with a spotter too and a waiver to f to fly beyond the line of sight which is really expensive and you can only go you know a kilometer or 2 km but actually I see that as really liberating for for that industry because those individuals can now do other high paid high skil jobs maintaining those systems being there as safety pilots if they do want you know need to intervene for some reason there will be safety pilots maybe telly operator whatever it might might be I don't think it's scary I think it's a really enabling thing but we we have to get away from this infrastructure and allow that to be on the system and to make it actually viable and that's the kind of use case that I think we're most likely to see early on um you know something like drone delivery, maybe not in inner cities, but perhaps uh logistically up and down the country following things like railway lines. Um so I flew a variety of different aircraft while I was in the Air Force but um my last frontline type was uh the A330 Voyager. Uh and and especially at night, I just found it interesting listening into what was going on. Uh and I I heard some guys getting into trouble. So, we were refueling some UK typhoons at the time and and we used to do something called dragging. So, we we drag them towards where they were going, which is something that that's very British. Uh we trying to help people out. It's why people like the British tankers, you know, we get them more time on their task and and sort of head in that direction. So, they they I think they stayed on task a little bit longer than they probably should have done to get something done. So, the fuel state was a little bit low. They'd gone to their tanker, the US tanker that had gone US. So, it broke. it was unserviceable. They'd gone to another one and amazingly that also broke and that left them in a in a situation where effectively they didn't have diversion fuel anymore. So their only option now was going to be head like towards the nearest safe place they could and eject over Iraq and Syria which as you can imagine is not a good uh a good solution. And you could hear the fear in their voices on the radio. So I I said to Mark like look can you can you handle all of this with the MSA? I'm just going to do some calculations cuz I've just heard something. I'll come back to you in a sec. And and this is where the team trust thing comes in, right? We're in the middle of, you know, I think we had probably four typhoons with us, you know, completely pitch black, uh just using the IR cameras to to do all this stuff over a war zone. And he's like, "Okay, cool. I'll come back to me." He just that trust is there that you're you're doing something that's important. So I went and ran some numbers on the fuel and went, "Okay, right. Um I think we can give them um you know, I think it was about four or five tons of fuel that we had spare. Uh, and if we head towards them, they're around 120 miles away, we get them coming towards us, and we get that coordinated, we might we might be able to stop them injecting. Uh, so I came back to Mark and went, "Hey, look, this is what's going on. We've got this much fuel. What do you think?" And he was like, "Yeah, I agree. We got to do it. We got to do it." So, uh, I I coordinated that. We we got heading towards these guys. You know, the Typhoon's like, "We'll take fuel and go." And the long and short of it is we we met up with those uh those guys in the in the dark in the middle of Syria. Uh they got on the hoses and and normally you would join the aircraft in a really safe way. You have to come up on the left do all these procedures to get behind the hoses. They were like we have to go straight for the hoses on night vision goggles which we weren't supposed to really do. And this is where you know sometimes you break rules because it's worth it and you can guarantee it's probably going to be safe. These guys got on the hoses and you could hear in their voices how scared they were and they were like guys we we had less than 30 seconds of fuel left when we got on your hoses. Like you've saved our lives. um like will be ever indebted to you for for what you did. Do we have the fuel to do it for ourselves? It came from our own fuel tank. So we had like limits of what we could do and predefined mission stuff. guys have planned to id8 and to talk through some things and then suddenly the conversation starts going into you know building new products hiring new resources or taking the company or taking the   16. @Breathflowconnection: The physiological sigh that Huberman talks about is something I use constantly in my facilitation work. Two short inhales through the nose followed by an extended exhale through the mouth. It works because the double inhale pops open the alveoli in your lungs that have collapsed, maximizing surface area for gas exchange. The long exhale then activates the parasympathetic nervous system through the vagus nerve. What I find in practice is that combining this with cold exposure creates a much deeper training effect. When someone is sitting in 3 degree water and their body is in full sympathetic activation, using breath control to deliberately shift into calm is the most powerful stress inoculation I have seen. The skill transfers directly to high pressure situations outside the water. After six years of running these sessions I still find it remarkable how fast people can shift their state once they understand the mechanism.

- How To Manage a Tech Team as a SaaS founder in 2025: Kind: captions Language: en If your dev team is sitting idle, confused, and you have no idea whether they are performing at their best, it can lead to costly mistakes and even put your SAS at risk. With Cat Talk, for example, we implemented systems to prevent wasted time through proper product and project management coaching, allowing their team to hit timelines for the first time ever. If you are seeing me for the first time, my name is Victor and I've worked with hundreds of non-technical founders and CEOs and this is the number one problem that keeps them up at night. So today, we'll find out how hiring and managing tech teams works. And finally, I will share with you my top secret tip to maximize your productivity and deliver software on time. Okay, let's start with mistake number one. Your developers are sitting around idle waiting for work. Then you assign a task, but suddenly you're getting 20 Slack messages asking for a clarification. Half your team is now waiting around for answers and progress is slow because obviously you are in an important sales call. There's a really simple fix for this, but first you need to understand why this happens. This is a symptom of a poorly defined work pipeline. the product vision isn't being broken down into clear, actionable, and well doumented tasks for the engineering team. Now, to solve this, first you need to maintain what we call a groomed backlog. Think of it like the chef's kitchen. A good chef doesn't just start cooking by running to the fridge, opening it up just to see what is in there. They all have their ingredients prepped, chopped, and ready to go. Your backlog, which is just your team's to-do list, needs to be the same. You should always have a list of the next five to 10 tasks clearly defined and ready to be picked up. This is the single most effective way to prevent developer downtime. Once you have the system up and running, you won't have those constant interruptions. Your developers know exactly what to build and you get to focus on actually growing instead of being everybody's chat GPT. It's honestly like getting your life back. And these are exactly the kinds of systems you need in place if you want to launch your MVP on time and without the usual chaos. If you'd like my personal help with that and setting this up and much more, there's a link in the description to just book a call with me. So, feel free to do that. Now, on to mistake number two. This feels like a very smart business move until you realize it's actually the most expensive decision you can possibly make. I'm talking about it feels very smart to hire three junior developers instead of one. I mean, hey, they might even cost the same because three devs will work more than one dev, right? But this is one of the most expensive and momentum killing mistakes you can possibly make. One problem is that you're not just paying their salaries. The true cost of an inexperienced developer includes the hours your senior team members have spent mentoring them, training them, and fixing their mistakes. The biggest slowdown in any project, by the way, isn't coding. It is communication and management overhead. So, your first technical hire needs to be strong and experienced because the person's main job isn't just to write code, but to build the entire foundation of the product and understand what it is that you want to build. You need someone who can work autonomously, make smart technical decisions, and ideally help you hire the next person. So, when you do hire, you need to look for what I call force multiplier skills. skills that make the whole team better during the interview. Screen for passion, for resourcefulness. Ask them about a difficult project they worked on. Do they light up when they talk about it? Ask them about a time they got stuck. Did they give up or did they just find a creative way to solve the problem? You want people who are teachable, humble, and not just technically brilliant. Also, make a plan. Plan for how your team policies and structure will scale as you grow. Don't just add team members without a plan. Now, all these tips will change your hiring mindset, knowing how who to hire and why, because now you aren't going for the most hours worked. You are investing in actual progress. One great engineer is worth five script kitties or more, much more actually, because they make the entire team better. You need quality, not just quantity. On to mistake number three, which I call the leadership vacuum. This is one where no one seems to be in charge of the product's direction and happens much more frequently than you might think. You thought your first developer whom you called a CTO would handle it, but they are more focused on code than on users. And developers more often than not aren't great product people or great people managers. And to understand the solution here, you need to first understand what each role really needs to even do. The product lead, what we call the product lead, is responsible for the what, the why, and in which order. They own the product roadmap. They talk to customers, they define features, and they prioritize what gets built next. And in the beginning, that person most likely has to be you. As the founder, you are the first product lead. You hold the vision. The tech lead is your most senior developer. They are responsible for the how. They own the technical architecture. They ensure the code is of high quality and they mentor other developers on the team. They take the what from the product lead and figure out the best way to then actually build it. The solution is to really from the beginning separate these two roles, the product lead and the tech lead. I'm not saying this is non-negotiable, but finding one person who really excels at both is like finding a unicorn and you're probably not there. Also, don't give the co title away for the to first hire like candy on Halloween. Founding engineer or tech lead is often a more appropriate title for a first tech hire because it doesn't imply the very highlevel people and you know money management skills. Now, doing this will give clear accountability for both product direction and technical execution. The developers will know who to ask about features and your product lead can make decisions him or herself. And now you've got a system that scales even without you making every tiny little decision, giving you the freedom to focus on growing the business while your product development runs smoothly. But if you're a non-technical founder, the thought of finding and vetting the right tech or product lead is probably a little bit terrifying. That's why I launched SAS Mastery. I give you advice around hiring the exact people you need and creating a fitting setup and we can source and prevet those candidates for you. So if you want to skip all those different problems non technical founders fall into while launching their MVP and you need the help of maybe an entire trusted team from day one, then click the first link in the description and let's talk on to mistake number four. As a non-technical founder, you have no real way of knowing if the work being produced is any good. I mean, you see task B marked as done, check, but you're not sure if the code is of a solid foundation or just a house of cards. You can't tell if the team is moving fast or just spinning its wheels. And this will also allow you to find the weak spots of your code because your team might be shipping features quickly, but cutting corners on code quality to meet deadlines. This will get your technical debt higher and higher and higher and make it act like boom, compound interest. And every new feature becomes just a little bit harder and a little bit more expensive to build. So to fix this, you can, for example, hire a fractional CTO for quarterly audits. For a few hundred or a couple thousand dollars, you can pay a highly experienced independent technical leader for a few hours of their time. They'll review your code, assess your team's progress and the process, and give you an unbiased report on the health of your technology. It's like getting a home inspection before you buy a house. It's like a no-brainer, right? And also don't get fooled by those many vanity metrics like lines of code written or tickets closed. These really mean absolutely nothing. The only metric that matters is shipping valuable features to your customers or even better measurable improvements and KPIs like feature adoption, churn, or user activation. You have to focus on outcomes not activity. Finally, and not many non-technical founders do this, God your team's sentiment. You can use simple or even anonymous weekly surveys asking them to rate their confidence in the project from 1 to five. Low morale is almost always a leading indicator of slow progress or deep technical problems. These things will allow you to have three unbiased sources of information on what's happening and allow you to make decisions about timeline, budget, and technical debt. And most importantly, you can catch problems very early when they're less time-conuming to fix rather than in the launch phase or funding round. And finally, this final mistake is what I call the Goldilocks problem. Your tech team is either building a massively scalable system designed for millions of users when you only have 10 or they've built such a cheap flimsy version that it's constantly breaking even though customers are coming in and coming in. One is too big, the other is too small. But both will ruin your business in different ways. The overengineered route burns through your oneway because you're paying for servers and complexity you don't need and waste your time on engineering it. The underengineered route means you you spend all your time putting out fires instead of actually growing the business. So your focus should be to build a solution that solves the core user problem at the scale you're currently at or will be in the near future. not building a system that handles millions, but also not something that is just held together with duct tape. And to help you stay on track, one of the most useful and overlooked tips is to simply ask why. If your developer wants to use some complex setup, ask why. If they say for scale, ask why you need that scale. What scale? What skill are we talking about? Keep asking why until you understand the real business reason. And finally, the most important technical feature for any startup and my top secret, it's the ability to change direction quickly. A simple system is easier to adapt and change. We all know the road to product market fit is littered with pivots, changes, uh new features that come up last minute. You need to maximize the speed of iteration. Great way to enforce this is by using a fixed time and budget with a flexible scope. You tell the team, "We have two weeks and this much money to solve this problem. Let's figure out the simplest version we can ship." By doing this, you've just unlocked your startup's greatest competitive advantage, speed. By building what's needed now, with the simple, reliable tools, you can adapt and respond to your customers faster than any of your bloated competitors. So, there you have it. The five biggest pitfalls for non-technical CEOs and the playbook to avoid them. We've just gone from chaos to a predictable workflow. You don't need to learn how to code to be an effective non- tech leader. You need a framework and the confidence to ask the right questions. This is your framework. Now, I know putting this into practice is the hard part. If you want an expert guide in your corner to make sure you get it right, that's exactly what I do. - 5 Rules for Successful Startup Pilots, POCs, Trials | Dose 045: Kind: captions Language: en welcome to the dream at dose many early stage startups are focused on running trials proofs of concept and pilots to help grow their pipeline frequently they don't get the results they're hoping for there are several critical issues you need to get right for these to be worth both your time and effort luckily there's a framework for this let's dive in dream adventures has been investing in great early stage startups for over a decade the dream at dose allows us to share best practices to help even more founders tell us about your great startup using the links below [Music] let's start by making sure we're on the same page regarding terminology first up and least formal let's talk about trials trials are usually when a customer implements your product in their environment with or without your assistance this provides them with hands-on experience and helps them validate features functionality and benefits next in formality would be a proof of concept or poc so it's a proof of concept right so what makes a poc different from a trial is adding a hypothesis this means that before starting a poc you should clearly define and agree to what you're trying to prove now let's go to the most formal of the three pilots what we're adding here beyond the hypothesis is strong purchase intent so the customer is agreeing in advance that if the pilot works out they'll be purchasing okay those are the three a trial which is the foundation poc which adds a hypothesis and a pilot that adds intent to purchase let's start getting into the top issues you should focus on to make sure your pocs and pilots deliver results first up clear success criteria frequently we hear founders pitch us and start talking about how they're doing pocs and pilots with several brand name companies everyone's excited but we'll come right back with great what are the success criteria so the poc or pilot is successful if what happens oh and the potential customer agreed to that this is usually followed up with a very muddled answer so rule number one of pocs and pilots have a short list of clearly defined and mutually agreed upon success criteria before starting and let's refine that further make those success criteria quantitative not qualitative so not a qualitative like we want to get patients out of the hospital faster but a quantitative statement like we want to decrease the time patients are in the hospital by 20 percent not we want to improve our incident response to a malware incident but instead we want to decrease our malware incident response time by 50 i mentioned having a short list of criteria some people use the term love metric what's a love metric this essentially means what's the one metric you would really love to see impacted the point is don't have too many success criteria focus on the ones that make the most difference to your potential customer last point is to be clear on the time frame to meet the success criteria is the poc or pilot running for a month three months or how long make sure the time frame is defined so round success criteria mutually agreed upon quantitative not qualitative define your love metric and clear time frame okay next let's talk about one of the best ways to reduce friction when trying to execute successful trials pocs and pilots do not integrate what do i mean oftentimes a customer will ask that you integrate your product or system with theirs avoid that if at all possible why we've seen time and again that integration during a poc or pilot is the wood chipper of startups why well now it needs to get involved oh wait they're busy that adds a delay oh nit now needs a security audit another delay and the list of delays goes on and on as your startup's runway diminishes so what do you do look carefully at your success criteria and ask how can you prove out what you need to without integrating can data be exported from the other system and import it into yours to prove things out or maybe your system delivers results but does not push them back into the customer's main systems via integration so one huge way to reduce friction and get faster results don't integrate if possible now let's talk about charging versus not charging in these early stages look if you're doing a trial in the user self-service you probably shouldn't charge but if you start raising the bar to a poc or pilot which means you'll be expending resources give charging strong consideration one of the biggest reasons you can quickly weed out how serious the potential customer is as an example if you're selling an enterprise solution that costs ten thousand dollars per month and the user is not willing to pay several thousand dollars to run a poc or pilot tread carefully their willingness to pay or wtp shows their level of seriousness it's a form of sales qualification want to throw in a sweetener tell them if they eventually buy you'll credit some or all of the charges to the full purchase price your call of course on offering that and since we're talking about money let's go a step further another big mistake we see startups make is not qualifying potential customers before starting pilots now i already mentioned a customer's wtp or willingness to pay for a poc or pilot this just makes sure that they're serious but you also need to make sure customers have an accurate view of the cost of your production solution post poc or pilot remember what's the point an eventual purchase i'm not saying you need to get a final negotiated contract and pricing before starting a poc or pilot but you want to make sure customers have visibility into your production use pricing and agree that it's within budget otherwise you risk wasting time and effort with a customer that really can't afford your solution i've seen several cutting-edge startups go a step further and ask potential customers to sign a provisional purchase order it's basically the letter of intent or loi that states if the pilot is successful they expect to purchase within a stated price range and time period the provisional purchase order typically does not have sharp teeth but does make sure your potential customer has their organization's commitment and is not wasting your time okay that's it those are five ways to make sure your trials pocs and pilots drive better outcomes first make sure you're using the right terms trial poc or pilot second make sure to have clearly defined success criteria that everyone agrees to and try to make them quantitative not qualitative third try to eliminate integrations unless that's part of your core value prop and you absolutely can't demonstrate success without it fourth think about charging for pilots to qualify potential customers finally before you start a pilot make sure production pricing has been discussed and think about getting provisional purchase orders that's it that's your dream of dose please leave your questions in the comment section if you found this video valuable please like and subscribe we have a lot more coming So So there's a lot of real world issues that we have to go solve, but the pure technology side is actually uh fairly evolved. >> Um you're the partner for the Olympics. >> Yes. >> So we're going to be seeing these fly for the Olympics. >> Oh yeah, that's the goal. So the Olympics was really a concept uh the LA28 Olympics was a concept that the FAA actually put out back in 2022. So they said they called it innovate 28. They said we want to see these aircrafts used in masses at one one city um at on kind of a big stage. And so they picked the LA28 games. Um and it also acted for the industry as a way to say by this date we need everything in place. We need not just the rules done but we need the infrastructure partners to you know to build uh the necessary charging equipment and takeoff and landing zones. We needed all the different supply chain partners uh to finish up all their work too and all of us coming together for one date to show these aircrafts at scale and I think uh from the arch perspective it should be an amazing show. >> Will you be actually like does that mean you're rolling out service and you you'll be uh I guess and I guess just as importantly what does that mean in terms of a business model? >> Yeah. Well, the goal is to move VIPs, athletes, and fans in and around the games. So, there's a whole network that's being designed around the Olympic venues, uh, around the airports to move people around, and I think it'll be a a way to showcase to the world, uh, you know, what these aircrafts can do with, you know, a lot of the global dignitaries, you know, uh, in one place. So, that'll be really exciting. But Archer does sell aircraft. That is our core business model. And so you know we will uh you know kind of use that also as a way to you know kind of showcase and advertise these aircrafts to all the potential future customers. >> Oh interesting. So you basically man manufacture you develop you manufacture and then you sell these aircraft to others that will roll out the services in different cities and whatnot. >> Yes. So I do think over the long term air taxi is an incredible opportunity and will come become very big but I think we have to be realistic on the timelines of how that will roll out. And so I think about it as you have to earn the right uh to to build a big air taxi network. And so we'll sell aircrafts as a way to generate revenue and cash flow and slowly build up the air taxi sort of the Archer network air taxi um over time. So it's likely something of a you know decade multi-deade long journey before there's real liquidity you know across the networks. This is an event like this is interesting because you get to see some of the different aircraft that are either under development or even potentially um competing in this emerging marketplace. How do you think about the competitive landscape? >> Um the good news is um you know the industry has largely consolidated to a few kind of core players and so it used to be a thousand and now there's kind of more like a handful and that allows a lot of the focus of the regulators to work on just a few. It allows the infrastructure partners to build standards, charging standards and uh you know just the different uh uh work that has to get done just around a few which allows it all to progress. And so I think the uh the industry has it's been competitive at times but it also is trying to come together um to as one to really build uh you know a more friendly environment to actually like launch these aircrafts. In example, Archer buys uh the charging equipment from Beta. Um so Beta is uh you know could be viewed as a competitor. I more view them as a friend. Um and so they're uh you know one of the partners that we're working with to help stand up the whole industry together. >> So how do you see this ecosystem evolving then? >> Um I think there will be a time to compete um and there will be a you know a time for uh for that but I think now um I think it is important that the industry does try to come together um and you know get these aircrafts ac across the finish line and build out this new ecosystem and um I think that's starting to happen. Um, but it's of course uh it's always competitive world out there, a competitive environment, but we're all trying >> commercial versus defense and other government applications. How are you thinking about that? >> So, a lot of what we talk about is the civil application because that's kind of the sexy thing where you can see these aircrafts and people can envision themselves flying around. It's like, h, who wants to drive 90 minutes to the airport? Manhattan to JFK, that's kind of brutal. What if I could fly there in five minutes? And you can see yourself doing that. Um but the defense applications I do think will actually be quite large and I think over the next decade you're going to see very significant uh defense uh applications for these types of aircrafts and the reason is we can build much lower cost aircrafts that are very capable of doing similar things that the existing rocraft fleet can do and new things that the rotocraft fleet can't do. um they have a low thermal signature, they have a low acoustic signature. And so when you really start to look at the benefits of what these aircrafts can do and then you pair that against the conflicts today like Ukraine where you're seeing drones they call autonomous and attitudable um vehicles kind of starting to really dominate those conflicts. It's very easy to understand how EV talls can become or hybrid versions of EV talls can become very important for the future conflicts >> and you're garnering some of those contracts on the government side. So today we have a partnership with Andreal, so a defense tech company based in Southern California. And so we've announced we're building a um a hybrid uh aircraft um that will be autonomous. And um you know, the partnership with Andrew really evolves around uh you know, trying to create this new category for defense and uh building that up >> Great. Well, Adam, I appreciate it. Adam Goldstein of Archer Aviation. It's great to speak with you. >> Appreciate it.
