- Things No One tell you about being a Pilot | Watch This Before You Become a Pilot: Kind: captions Language: en Everyone sees the uniform, the wing, the views from 30,000 ft, but no one talks about this. These are some of the brutal truths that nobody tells you about. Here's 15 things no one tells you about being a pilot. Let's talk about it. You'll question yourself a lot. Did I make the right choice? Am I good enough? Can I really do this? There will be days when you mess up. There will be days when you feel like you're not enough. Days where you feel like everybody else is ahead of you. Days when your confident absolutely tinks. Imposttor syndrome does not skip pilots. In fact, it is one of our best friends. But here's what no one tells you. Even the best doubt themselves. So you are not alone in this. Do not feel like you are not enough. What matters is how you respond. You get up, debrief yourself, and keep moving ahead. That's the making of a real pilot. Your training never stops. You think you're dead after you pass your check ride? Think again. Recurrency sims, maneuvers, validation, emergency drills, and re-qualification exams. Your training never ends. You're constantly being evaluated, refreshed, and retested. But it's actually a good thing. Wouldn't you rather be safer by doing all these tests than be complacent and get hurt and hurt other people in the process? So these are extremely important and also help you stay on top of it because the day you stop learning, you're not just a pilot, you are a liability in the sky. Before we get into the most brutal truth of this video, I wanted to address a question that I keep getting very often. How to become a commercial pilot in the US if you are an international student. If you want to go from where you are today to becoming a commercial airline pilot in the US, I'm going to be hosting a webinar to tell you exactly how to do it because it is possible. I was where you are just a couple years ago and today I'm a commercial airline pilot in the US. If you want to find out how to do this, how much it costs, the hidden costs that nobody tells you about, the visa processes, what kind of flight schools to look for, and a step-by-step guide on how to go from where you are today, to becoming a commercial airline pilot, either in the US or in your own country. This is going to be the most comprehensive, best career tip that you will ever receive. Check out the link in the description to join the weight list for the webinar. Now, let's move on to the most difficult and hardest truth about being a pilot that nobody tells you. Loneliness hits different at 3:00 a.m. There's a kind of silence in hotel rooms that echoes when your body clock is flipped, your friends are asleep, and the only thing keeping you company is the buzz of the mini fridge in the corner. Yes, you're surrounded by people all day, but sometimes flying can feel isolating. You'll crave real connection, the kind that you have with friends and family, but you can't have because you're always gone. And in those quiet hours, you come face to face with yourself. Sometimes that can be powerful. Other times, it can be revealing of who you really are and what you're missing out. Being a pilot will test you mentally, emotionally, and financially. Flight school is expensive. We all know that. Airline upgrades aren't always linear. It doesn't always work out the way you plan. Schedules are brutal and the mental load heavy heavy. You'll cry. You'll doubt yourself. You'll burn out. And hopefully, you'll bounce back up, too. Each time that happens, you only become stronger, sharper, wiser. Because the ones who make it here are not the smartest ones or the richest ones. The ones who make it here are simply the ones that do not quit. You'll miss important moments. Birthdays, anniversaries, weddings. You might miss them all. You'll send I miss you texts from hotel rooms, FaceTime into family dinners, and watch life milestones just pass you by while you're in a city that you didn't even want to be in the first place. No one tells you that while you're chasing clouds in the sky, life on the ground moves on with or without you. You learn to be present in the absence. You learn to love from afar, to show up in small but meaningful ways, and most importantly to forgive yourself for the moments that you're not there for. But nonetheless, the sacrifice stings, especially when it's your niece's first steps, and you are missing it because you're in a city you didn't even want to be in the first place. Layovers aren't vacations. Yes, you can see new places, but not always. Sometimes the only sightseeing you get is the trip from the airport to the hotel in the crew van. Sometimes your layover is a 4-hour nap and a soggy sandwich that you have to grab on the go to not be late for your flight. It's not all glamorous like you see on your curated Instagram feed. But if you're intentional, you can definitely carve out little pockets of joy in every little trip you make and find ways to enjoy it and find moments of happiness in it. It just takes effort and willingness to make the most out of your small layover or a long labor, whatever you're getting. Your circle will shrink. You can't text your friends as much. People will stop inviting you to things, assuming you won't be around. Some might even resent your lifestyle, your schedule, and the fact that you're always somewhere else. But here's the bright side. The ones who stay are your people. It will show you who your girlfriends are and who really matter and who will stick with you till the end. Your circle might get smaller, but it will also get stronger, tougher, built on truth, not convenience. Relationships get tested. Romantic platonic family, this job, it stretches them all. You learn the value of trust, of setting expectations, of small gestures that show them that you care. Because being gone doesn't mean being distant, but it does mean working harder to stay connected. The ones who get it, they'll stay. The ones that don't, they'll drift away. Unfortunately, that's the reality of this job. You will never look at the sky the same way. Clouds are no longer just puffy little things. They become stories and a magnifying glass to what lays ahead. Because the sky becomes a second language, the one only pilots speak fluently. And even on the toughest days, you get to see views out of your office window that most people will never get to see in their entire lives. Views that I cannot put into words to explain just how beautiful they are and I cannot capture. So they forever live in my memories and mine alone. And I cannot show anybody just how beautiful this world is. You learn how to stay calm when everything is chaotic. When an engine alert pops up. When ATC reroutes you last minute. When you're flying into weather and every passenger is gripping their armrest. Your job is to be the calmst person in the room. Well, in the sky. It's not just about controlling the aircraft. It's about controlling your mind. Calm is powerful and it can save lives. Flight school isn't glamorous. You'll fly old, beat up planes in weird weather and with no air conditioning. There will be days with backto-back lessons, sweaty headsets, engine run-ups in the blazing sun, and debriefs that'll leave your ego extremely bruised. But one day when you look back, you'll miss it. The purity of chasing your wings, the passion you had that brought you to this point. So at the end of the day, it is all worth it. Weather will be your best friend and your worst enemy. It's your constant companion. You learn how to read mears and taps and all the gibberish of letters and numbers and combinations will actually start making sense. And sometimes it'll betray you. Out of nowhere, storms will shift and things that you didn't expect to happen will happen. And it might force you to divert, cancel, or delay flights. It humbles you. It reminds you even though you can read all this, have an amazing airplane, nature holds all the power. You'll feel behind even when you're not. Everyone has an opinion on your timeline. You'll see your classmates get jobs before you do. Friends hitting milestones when you are stuck in the same spot. But aviation is not linear. It doesn't always work the same way. Some paths are slower. Some paths are more windy. Some paths are just different. But none of them are wrong. As long as you're moving forward, even if it is one knot at a time, you're not behind. You are making progress and you are moving ahead. The job market isn't always fair. You could do everything right and still miss out and get skipped on a job opportunity. Timing, company politics, economy, and let's not forget the pandemic. Everything can change what is going to happen. It is not always about talent. A lot of time it's simply about timing and that can hurt. But it also teaches you resilience. It teaches you to stay ready so you don't have to get ready when the door finally opens and the opportunity arrives. Number 15 is you'll fall in love with it all over again. After all the hard days, even after the missed birthdays, bad sim session, and lonely nights in hotel rooms in unfamiliar cities, there will be moments, quiet, breathtaking moments when you remember exactly why you picked this job and why you love this job. Watching the sunrise through the flight deck window never gets old. The soft tud of the wheels after you absolutely grease a landing. Such an exhilarating feeling. The look on a little kid's face when you walk by or they see you through the flight deck door and they're just waving at you. That is why you're here. That is the feeling that this job inspires and you'll fall in love again and again and again because flying is hard and it's challenging, but it's also nothing short of magic. If you're still watching this video, maybe this job is for you. Because despite knowing the brutal truths that nobody tells you, if you're still here, maybe you have that resilience in you, the I don't quit attitude that is required to succeed in this job. So, keep going. We'll see you in the sky. Which of these facts surprise you the most? Let me know in the comments below. And I'll see you in my next video. Until then, signing off, Lightning Girl. but I was starting Flight Training as a former flight attendant someone who' already had a career I was in my late 20s I started flight training at 28 years old and I was not in the position where I a full-time program and the school wants your full-time availability so any kind of job where you have to have a dedicated schedule or hours that are scheduled in advance uh is just not going to work it's not a good fit for this type of program so the more that you can take on gig work or freelance things that you can do on your own time and aren't on a set schedule that is the best way to the FBO is the fixed Base operator it's just the general aviation terminal so there's usually a small team that works there so they are the ones that you're going to want to meet first if you're   1. @chrisbruffett3270 [6 likes]: I’m in SW MO. It took me 7 months and $13k to get my PPL last year at a 141 school. Took the check ride at 56 hours. However, I studied on my own for a year before walking in to the school. I took the written the 1st week there and got a 95%. Of course I still had to do their ground courses but my instructor flew through it since I already knew it. That was an added expense, but was still a savings vs them teaching me. Crappy spring weather slowed down my training as well. I had 4 flight days a week scheduled but only flew 3 times each month in Feb, Mar and April. Training was in a Piper Warrior at $84/hr dry, $60/hr fuel, $58/hr instruction.

If you have the means, this is really the best way to do it. The other thing that seriously helps is having a job that allows for free time during the week. I essentially have Monday-Thursday off due to my weird job…and that’s just about perfect for something like flight training. The few times I flew on Sunday during primary were kind of nightmarish…full patterns at every local field, practice areas all jammed up…weekends can be kind of brutal. What it boils down to is that your time/money situation has to be pretty ideal if you’re going to get flight training done in the best way humanly possible.

- Why SR-71 Blackbird Pilots Were Required to Have Families!: Kind: captions Language: en The reason state route 71 pilots were required to have a family was not technical but psychological. Flying the world's fastest and highest jet meant extreme danger, secrecy, and stress. The US Air Force believed that men with families were more stable, responsible, and less likely to take reckless risks. A family gave them balance, emotional strength, and a strong reason to stay grounded while performing one of the most dangerous jobs of the Cold War. Hello everyone, welcome back to the World Conflict Monitor channel. Please subscribe to my channel for more in-depth explanations of the world's most mysterious aircrafts, wars, and defense secrets. Today, we are going to uncover a fascinating question. Why did State Route 71 pilots have to have a family? At first, this requirement may sound unusual, even strange. Why would a country like the United States, which had the most advanced aircraft in the world, care about whether its pilots were married or had children? But once we go deeper into the story, the reasoning becomes very clear and it reveals how dangerous, sensitive, and unique the State Route 71 Blackbird really was. The state route 71 Blackbird is one of the most legendary aircraft in aviation history. Designed during the cold war by Lheed skunk works division under the genius of Clarence Kelly Johnson, this jet could fly at speeds above Mach 3 and at altitudes exceeding 85,000 ft. Its purpose was to spy on enemy nations without being caught. It could outrun missiles, radar, and even enemy interceptors. To fly this aircraft was both an honor and a burden because it demanded absolute perfection from its pilots. And that is where the family requirement comes in. The United States Air Force believed that only men with families could handle the mental stress of flying the most secretive and dangerous missions of the Cold War. They needed people who were not only technically skilled but also emotionally grounded. A family meant responsibility, stability, and maturity. From the exterior, the State Route 71 Blackbird looked like something from the future. Its sleek, sharp, needle-like nose, long fuselage, and wide wingspan gave it an almost alien appearance. Built primarily from titanium, it was resistant to the extreme heat caused by flying at three times the speed of sound. The exterior was coated in black paint mixed with radar absorbing iron ferite particles which reduced its radar cross-section and gave it its famous nickname, the Blackbird. Every panel, every rivet, and every joint was designed with precision because at such high speeds, even the smallest imperfection could tear the aircraft apart. For the pilot, sitting in front of this aircraft on the runway was like staring at the edge of human engineering. and the Air Force wanted only the most trustworthy individuals with strong character to sit inside. On the interior, the cockpit was very different from what many might expect today. It was compact, tight, and filled with analog gauges, dials, and switches. The environment was not designed for comfort. It was designed for survival. The pilots wore full pressure suits similar to astronaut suits complete with helmets and oxygen supply systems. Because at altitudes above 80,000 ft, the human body could not survive without them. Communication was limited and visibility was narrow. Inside the cockpit, the pilot and reconnaissance systems officer had to work in complete synchronization. Missions often lasted several hours, and during that time, there was no room for distraction. That is why the Air Force wanted pilots who were stable, disciplined, and emotionally balanced. Qualities that came naturally from having a family life back home. The short interior story is simple. The cockpit was like a space capsule and only someone with a stable. Personal life could handle that isolation and pressure. When we look at performance, the State Route 71 was unmatched in its era. It could cruise at speeds of Mach 3.2, two, meaning it traveled more than 2,000 mph. At this speed, the aircraft's skin would heat up to over 600° F. Most aircraft would melt at this temperature, but the Blackbird was built to endure it. Its engines, the Pratt and Whitney J58 turbo jets, were a marvel of engineering. They worked both as traditional jet engines and as ramjets at higher speeds, giving the aircraft unmatched performance. It could fly across continents in just a few hours, taking detailed photos of enemy installations without ever being shot down. In fact, no State Route 71 was ever lost to enemy fire. This performance made it the crown jewel of American reconnaissance. But it also meant that the pilots were constantly pushing the limits of what was humanly possible. That level of stress required not only skill but also mental resilience and family was considered the anchor for that resilience. Safety features in the state route 71 were not about comfort, they were about survival. The pilot's pressure suits doubled as full life support systems, keeping them alive if they had to eject at extreme altitudes. The cockpit had specialized systems to handle the rapid changes in pressure and temperature. Even the ejection seats were designed to function at Mach 3 speeds, something no other aircraft had attempted. But despite all these measures, the risk was always there. If anything went wrong at 80,000 ft, survival was uncertain. That is why the Air Force looked for pilots who understood risk, who respected responsibility, and who had families to return to. A man with a family was seen as less likely to take reckless chances and more likely to make sound decisions under stress. The unique selling points of the State Route 71 Blackbird were clear, unmatched speed, altitude, and stealth for its time. It could outrun any missile, fly higher than any enemy aircraft, and gather intelligence with cameras so advanced that they could read license plates from 80,000 ft. But beyond technology, its uniqueness also lay in the pilots who flew it. These were not ordinary men. They were carefully selected, highly trained, and required to have families to ensure their psychological stability. In other words, the state route 71 program did not just rely on engineering. It relied on human reliability. And that human factor is what truly set the Blackbird apart. When we talk about price, the State Route 71 was incredibly expensive. Each aircraft cost around $34 million in the 1960s, which would be hundreds of millions in today's dollars. Its operations and maintenance were also extremely costly, requiring specialized fuel, titanium parts, and highly skilled crews. The Air Force invested not only money, but also trust in the men who flew it. That trust is why the family requirement existed. It was not just about the machine. It was about ensuring the pilot was someone dependable, someone grounded in responsibility, and someone who valued life enough to avoid unnecessary risks. In conclusion, the requirement for State Route 71 pilots to have families was not random. It was a carefully thoughtout decision by the United States Air Force. The State Route 71 Blackbird was not just an aircraft. It was a weapon of intelligence, a guardian of national security, and a symbol of Cold War power. To fly, it meant putting your life at risk on every mission, handling extreme stress, and carrying the weight of national secrets. The Air Force believed that only men with families could truly handle that responsibility. Because a family gave them balance, purpose, and the mental strength to survive the most dangerous job in aviation. And that is why state route 71 pilots had to have a family. This explanation shows us that behind every piece of technology, no matter how advanced, there are human beings. The state route 71 was fast, powerful, and nearly invincible. But its success depended on pilots who were grounded in their humanity. And sometimes being grounded simply meant having a family to go home to.

- How To Become a Pilot (Step-By-Step Guide): Kind: captions Language: en career if you want to get paid to fly you will need at least a second class medical certificate and if you plan to become an airline pilot you will need a first class medical I recommend that you get one of those prior to starting Flight Training to prevent any surprises down the line most people pass the medical exam without an issue but some medical conditions can prevent you from becoming a pilot one of the most common concern is color vision deficiency now Color Blind Pilots can still fly but they're usually restricted to Flying recreationally and only during the day so if you want to make a career out of flying you will need to pass a color vision test if you're concerned about color blindness or other health conditions we've linked additional information in the description below your Amame also can address some of the concerns you may have we've also added a link down here to help you find an am near you okay so you meet the medical requirements what's next well you need to choose a flight school this is one of the most important decision you will make in your flying career if you make the wrong choice it could end your dream entirely here's what you need to know about selecting a flight school the first thing to consider is whether to go for a part 61 or part 141 flight school basically there are two types of flight schools and different rules apply to them in general part 61 schools offer more flexible training programs on the other hand part 141 schools are more rigid and structured you can get your license faster at a part 141 flight school but they are more expensive to learn more check out this video right here where I explain the difference in detail another consideration is the distance between your home and the flight school now keep in mind that your commute could add a significant amount of time to your training so plan accordingly next consider the type of airplane that the flight school operates the most important thing make sure that the airplanes are in good condition and wellmaintained newer aircraft are an advantage especially if they have glass cockpits but you don't need to fly the latest and greatest to get your license there's definitely a premium to newer aircraft step three after you choose a flight school you'll need to choose an instructor a lot of students don't even think about choosing a fight instructor this is a big mistake the wrong instructor can make or break your flight training experience here's how to make the right choice first make a list of the flight schools near you visit a few of them and then speak with the instructors are they professional enthusiastic welcoming that's a great sign a happy instructor is a good instructor or do the instructors look like they haven't slept in day and maybe wear a permanent frown well you might want to look elsewhere in this case now consider doing an intro flight at several flight schools this is the first flight you will have before officially beginning your training it usually consists of a scening route with an instructor and it's a great way to get a good sense of how flight school operates it's also a good idea to take multiple intro flights with several instructors at different flight schools before you make a choice also don't hesitate to request different instructors early on if you feel like they're not a good fit once you've chosen your flight school and instructor it's time to start Flight Training now this can be a little bit scary especially if you don't know what you expect now let's talk about the structure of a typical flight lesson first your instructor will give you an overview of the lesson it's very important that you prepare four lessons ahead of time then you'll both head out to the airplane what you do during the flight depends on the lesson but there is a general structure your instructor will typically demonstrate a procedure or maneuver then you will try to do it on your own now you'll continue doing that until they are happy with your performance now I expect a postflight briefing at the end of the lesson you're a new instructor will discuss your performance what you did well and also what needs Improvement this is also a good time to learn about your next flight now with each flight and ground lesson you get closer to earning your private pirate certificate the one that will allow you to fly with passengers without getting paid when your instructor is confident in your abilities they'll endorse you for for your first solo which is exactly what it sounds like you will be flying the airplane on your own for the first time very exciting after you start flying solo it's time to think about taking your knowledge test now this is the first official test that you will need to take so what does the test consist of well the Private Pilot Knowledge Test has 60 multiple choice questions you will have 2 hours to complete it and you need to score 70% or higher so here's what you should do to pass with flying colors the key is prepar ation don't expect to read just a few books and then go pass the test now luckily that's where we can help our Private Pilot Made Easy online Ground School is designed to help you pass the exam we're so confident that we'll even send you $175 and a full refund if you don't pass on the first try now click on the link in the description to learn more you're almost there after you've passed your Knowledge Test the only thing left is an in-person test with a designated pilot examiner or DPE DPE is an exper examiner approved by the FAA their job is to make sure that you are qualified proficient and a safe pilot this in-person test is also known as a practical test or more commonly a check ride the check ride has an oral and a flight portion the oral exam happens on the ground and consists of a conversation between you and the DPE they're going to evaluate your Aviation knowledge your flight planning and the decision- making skills it can range anywhere between an hour and a half to 3 hours now when your DPE is happy with your knowledge it's time to hop in the aircraft during the flight portion you will demonstrate various skills Maneuvers and that usually takes about an hour the DP wants to see you operate the aircraft safely and with Precision it will also test your decision-making abilities in the aircraft if the DP is happy with your skills you will become the country's newest pilot congratulations this is the first step on the way to becoming an airline pilot so how long does it take to get your private pirate certificate well if you're training full-time can be around 3 to 6 months you'll need a minimum of 40 hours of flight time but the average person takes about 70 to 80 hours it really depends on the weather the type of airspace that you learn in how consistently you fly and in many other factors okay so you're pilot but you can't get paid to fly just yet before you get your commercial pilot certificate you need to obtain flying experience and an instrument rating here's how an instrument rating allows you to fly an aircraft in limited visibility using only the instruments in the cockpit similar to the private pattern process you'll need to pass a written and a practical test good news if your current fly instructor can provide instrument training you can continue to fly with them the experience is pretty much the same as your Private Pilot training just more complex Concepts after you get your instrument rating you're almost there the bad news well now you need to get a commercial pirot certificate the good news the process is very similar to your Private Pilot certificate there's a knowledge test and a check ride that follows the same format we just discussed the biggest difference is the level of difficulty the commercial Knowledge Test covers much more than the private pirate test and the check ride is way more strict but what makes the commercial certificate especially difficult is how much experience you need for starters you'll need at least 250 hours of flight time now at a part 141 flight school you only need 190 hours but it still takes a significant amount of time and money to achieve once you pass the commercial check ride you can finally get paid to fly if you train full-time you can expect to obtain your commercial certificate in about 6 to 18 months keep in mind that this can vary depending on the weather and the flight scool now it's time to go straight to the airlines right well not exactly before you fly the big jets you need more hours under your belt that's why many commercial pilots become flying structures it's a fantastic way to quickly gain experience at this point you should also get your multi-engine tring this allows you to fly aircraft with more than one engine But ultimately you need an airline transport pilot certificate in order to fly for an airline and that requires 1,500 hours of flight time now many inspiring airline pilots gain those hours by instructing some Pilots also find other commercial flying jobs that don't require an ATP many do a bit of both once you've earned that ATP certificate your dream is finally Within Reach and now you can start to apply for Airline jobs it can take anywhere from 2 to 5 years in order to obtain that ATP certificate depending on the type of flying that you do now you may say what is this going to cost Well Medical certificate can cost anywhere between $75 to $200 depending on the type of certificate and also the doctor your Private Pilot certificate will average $ 15 to $20,000 this also varies significantly depending on the flight school type of aircraft the instructor or even the location the commercial pilot certificate is the most expensive expect around $55,000 this can even go into six figures depending on the flight school you should also consider the cost of obtaining your flight instructure certificate now this is not a requirement but it's one of the best way to gain experience before you go to the airlines a fly instructor rating will cost around $5,000 the good news is that once you are employed as a fly instructor or you can start to get paid to fly you flight school might even pay for additional upgrades to your instructor rating and finally your ATP certificate some Airlines might pay for your ATP but if you have to pay for it yourself it will cost around $5,000 now I know you're probably a little intimidated at this stage by all of this information but guess what hundreds of thousands of people just like you have become Pilots we even had an interview with one of our students who completed his For generations we make vodka, make vodka out of potato, rye and wheat, and we sell it all over the world. The distillery where we are is about two hours outside of Warsaw. It started in 1896 and we make vodka in the same location for 120 years. I'm trying to be here as often as possible, especially at this time of the year, to talk to the farmers, to check if the potatoes are ready, if this is a time to already take them out of the ground and distill them. We start in September, it's 24 hours a day, seven days a week, and then we store the product and we can then process it and make vodka out of it for the rest of the year. Then obviously as we make it here - and we make it in Poland - that product travels around the world, so we need to be in the places where the product is, and it's nice that you could actually combine being in the morning in the fields, before lunch, somewhere in the city and before dinner in another city. For that one you really need to move very fast between the area - that's what HondaJet is all about. It's about helping you to do many more things. I met Mr. Fujino and listened to his story about the passion, how he built the plane, and we started to talk and I told him this story how I started to make vodka and my passion for making vodka, and something clicked, and we said well you know we are such different businesses but we have something in common, and that something in common is passion for what we do. I was invited to Greensboro to have a look at it when the plane was being built, before it was painted, and started to be familiar with it. I decided to talk about it and and look at it some more. I have to say I'm very nicely surprised. It doesn't feel like a small jet at all. I use HondaJet for business and pleasure, and the reason for that is that our business is in the nicest hotels, nicest resorts, the nicest restaurants. We go there to do business and we also go to the same places for leisure. And HondaJet allows me to do that, allows me to do it very efficiently. The nice hotels or resorts are not always close to the airport but maybe they are close to some other small airport where the HondaJet can land. So you can get to those spaces without changing planes. It's all about moving as efficiently through space and time as you possibly can. You have to have a human being behind it. You have to have somebody whose passion overcomes all of the difficulties. It looks easy maybe later when it succeeds but the beginnings are always difficult. And that's what it is, about HondaJet, it's built out of passion. Chopin Vodka is built out of passion. And we are changing those categories, we are changing the way that people perceive them, the way that people look at those products. I'm trying to make the best vodka in the world, and HondaJet is aspiring to be the best jet in the world.

a revolutionary new flying machine invented by brothers wilbur and orville wright orville took his position in the cradle wilbur was on the right wing they started the engine and behold 40 feet down the rail the aircraft lifts up they flew 120 feet in 12 seconds 12 seconds that changed the history of mankind forever the successful test of the wright brothers flying machine was a watershed moment in human history the 12-second flight proved that sustained controlled powered flight was in fact possible for the wright brothers this achievement was the fulfillment of a childhood dream one of the most extraordinary things about the rights is the fact that they were two ordinary people from a middle class family in ohio [Music] their father was a bishop and he was a voracious reader and so there were books in the right household of all sorts their mother was a sort of mechanical whiz she loved fixing things building them toys and so they got a sort of taste from her a kind of mechanical taste the story of the wright brothers actually started 25 years before kitty hawk when they were seven and 11 years old when their father brought home the toy flying machine built by a frenchman alfonso panel this was powered by force of a twisted rubber band and that really amazed the wright brothers because they had never seen a toy flying machine in their lives being curious they played with it and they broke it but they didn't just throw it away first they repaired it but then they started to reproduce the toy themselves the wrights owned the right cycle company in in dayton ohio and they were bicycle salesmen uh and and repairmen they were always curious technically minded motivated young people who built a bicycle shop and made a profit in order to put money into test flights at one point wilbur says the problem of flight is actually a thousand problems first you have to raise the money to solve the problem well that's a very different disposition than the technical mind who can figure out the uh the equations for air pressure and lift and these sorts of things in 1899 the wright brothers performed their first flight experiment by testing a rudimentary kite flyer they also looked to nature for clues on how to solve the problem of power flight they saw all these examples in nature of flight the butterfly the dragonflies the buzzards and vultures and they found that there were two methods of flight the fast wing beaters and then the sauron birds they patted their machine after a particular bird the turkey vulture which was a very good soaring bird they would read books about animal locomotion and look at bird's wings and people were like what are those guys doing but they were looking at the way the birds twist their wings because neither of them went to college the rights benefited from breaking the chains that might have otherwise have bound their minds the wright brothers took these unconventional observations of animals flying in nature and applied them to their flight experiments this innovative approach would prove to be a stroke of genius the major breakthrough that the wright brothers discovered was the curvature of the wing has a dramatic effect on the ability to produce lift wilbur one day was in the bicycle shop and he held a bicycle inner tube box and as he twisted the ends of the box he had an aha moment and realize that the box ends move very much like the wingtips of the birds he integrated that idea into what he called wing warping in the wing design that we see on the 1903 right flyer the wright brothers always said when people asked them what got you interested in flight what made it such that you wanted to solve the problem of flight why did you persevere through all your failures they would say we did it for fun we were having fun and then they would say we were frustrated that a lot of smart people thought flight should work and it didn't work and we wanted to know why and we wanted to fix it the wright brothers incredible innovations allowed them to solve the centuries-old mystery of powered flight in fact so much of what we take for granted today from commercial airlines space shuttles can be traced back to these two ordinary brothers and their unlikely stroke of genius there were many people before the rights who attempted to create flying machines you can think about people like leonardo da vinci you can think about edison who was interested in the problem alexander graham bell the genius of the wright brothers is what they had was a gumption to say why not NOT the wright brothers. A prototype aeroplane using electroaerodynamic propulsion flew for 12 seconds, the same as the Wright brothers achieved with their maiden flight. 
It is a historical fact that the Wright brothers flew their bird-like monoplane on 17 December 1903. But New Zealander Richard Pearse was the first person to fly, as early as 31 March 1902, more than 18 monthsbefore the Wright brothers.
Later, witnesses reported that Pearse flew 400 metres and soft-landed on a hedge on his Timaru farm. His aircraft was also the first to use proper ailerons, instead of the inferior warping system that the Wrights used.

How did the new space industry move so much faster than the legacy aerospace industry? in my career, I've worked on commercial aircraft. have hardware on the 777 and on the 747. And then after I left Boeing, I went over to the rocket industry and when I was building Terran 1 at Relativity Space. I started to realize that all of the processes that we were using, the tools and the technologies and everything, directly can be translated between new space and aviation. The aviation industry is slow and cautious, which is a good thing in a lot of ways. But it's also led us to where we are today, where there's two players who have kind of gobbled up everyone else in the market, and neither of those companies can deliver products to schedule or can really deliver any innovation. And the whole world, quite seriously, is hurting from it. Everyone, four billion people a year, get on passenger airplanes. And that's four billion people a year where higher ticket prices affect them, where travel delays, where cancellations, and everyone has experienced this at some point or another, almost. When we started looking at some of the new manufacturing technology that was out there, we realized that we had a chance right now to adopt it before any of the other primes could, because we have some pretty good insights on how to combine the rapid prototyping with more enduring, with materials like composites and with some other more reliable building methods. So the biggest one of the biggest concepts that I kind of picked up in the space industry is this idea of software defined tooling, where instead of in the past, you'd have really big like fixed tooling, big giant jigs or molds that, you know, are each built that take, you know, months to over a year to build some time and cost hundreds of millions of dollars. The space industry, in particular, relativity adopted using the started using these very large robots for 3D printing. And so they could print any kind of arbitrary shape on their printers. And I was watching them print, I watched them print like the nose cone and the fuel tanks and the fuel domes and barrels on the same machine without, know, and all they changed was a few lines of code. while we're not 3D printing the rocket, we started to realize, well, this kind of concept of how you are, how you're like putting all of your complexity into sort of the robots and into the software that actually like builds parts like molds and jigs and tools. That is a monumental cost savings that allows you to build really exciting, really experimental and very exotic shapes with your airplanes. And there's sort of a, dawning of this idea that came to me over the, over a couple of weeks at a time at a relativity where I realized that, wow, this technology lets us build exotic aircraft that current manufacturing just can't quite, produce reliably and, and repeatably at scale. So how does that make you guys different? What are you building? So our airplane is a mid-market passenger airliner. It's a 767, 757 size replacement. But we are using a blended wing body profile and we're bringing in modern, higher performance electronics and avionics systems that have been developed and proven out on launch vehicles like Falcon 9 and Starship and Terran 1. By doing that, we're able to build a plane that has the range and payload of a 767, but does it with the fuel consumption of a 757. So we're burning about half of the fuel of the large widebody aircraft that we replace. Yes, that's huge. So sustainability, big, you know, is that an important factor for you guys then? It is a lot. Immediately we have this huge reduction in fuel burn, but other parts of it we want to remove. There's other things that like help sustainability and aviation. We're moving towards more recyclable materials and more reusable processes. Carbon fiber production has tons of waste in material supplies and scrap and a Consumables And so part of what our technology does is it allows us to actually like reduce the amount of consumables and the amount of waste in the composites manufacturing process. And then because we're printing our molds out of recyclable parts, we can actually go ahead and after we've, you know, We've built a mold and if we need to scrap it or get rid of it, we can shred it and reuse it in the same kind of printing process, which is kind of a cool longer term thing that we're looking at. very cool. This is the solution. these are the financials, metrics, business model, etc., etc., and you go, you send it to investors and make it look nice and hope that they reply to you. You have a few meetings with them. Um, and you just get through the gist of actually being able to sell to investors. And this is what most people don't I wouldn't say they necessarily know is that trying to sell to an investor is the same way that you would try and sell to a client. You need to do the exact same thing or that you need to use the same resources to be able to do that. So, you know, why should I give why should I invest in your business? You know, why should I um have you thought about this? Where's your financial model? Where's your um I don't know the the revenue forecast, whatever it may be that they need to ask for. You need to have that ready and an answer ready for that. Um and for most questions, you're not going to have an answer ready because you've only just met the guy and you've never been asked this question before. So that's why investment takes such a long time as well is because you're trying to refine your idea and your narrative of what your business is, but also at the same time you're trying to the investors will ask you the same question again and again and again and maybe only on the fifth time you answer that question will you get it right. Will that be enough to actually satisfy the investor? - How Flight Simulators & Tech Are Transforming Pilot Training 🚀: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Day 10, the role of aviation technology in flight training. Hello again. Today we're diving into the exciting world of aviation technology and how it's revolutionizing flight training. In today's digital age, technology is changing the way we learn to fly. Tools like flight simulators, electronic flight bags, and advanced navigation software are making flight training more efficient, safer, and accessible. Flight simulators are one of the most significant innovations. These realistic tools allow you to practice maneuvers, emergency scenarios, and instrument approaches without ever leaving the ground. You can work on your skills in various weather conditions, scenarios, and giving you valuable practice before you ever even enter the airflight. Additionally, EFBs like forflight are transforming flight planning and navigation. With these tools, you can plan flights, check weather conditions, and track your routes in real time, all from the tab, all from your tablet or smartphone. This not only makes flying more convenient, but also helps you stay safer by keeping you informed throughout your flight. At Jet with Joe, we integrate these technologies into your training program and lessons to make sure you get the most out of every training session. Whether you're using a simulator uh to learn a procedure or an app to plan a cross-country flight, these tools are here to help you succeed faster and more safely. Stay tuned as we continue to explore the future of aviation and the tools that will make you a better pilot.

started I need to make one thing abundantly clear you must have your FAA part 107 certification to fly drones commercially no exceptions that's something you'll have to get before working as a professional drone pilot number one agricultural drone pilot agriculture is a specialized application of drone technology that stands out due to its requirement for a multi-spectral sensor this Advanced sensor detects light and the non-visual range particularly infrared and near infrared allowing it to collect data indicative of crop Health by using drones equipped with these sensors to capture data over Farmland farmers can generate in DVI Maps or normalize difference vegetation index enabling effective crop Health Management identifying areas with sub-optimal health enables precise resource allocation like irrigation fertilizers and pesticides and more while it's a relatively recent addition to agriculture entering this field through drone Services can place you at the Forefront of this Innovative Trend number two mapping and surveying creating 3D maps and models through drone technology holds broad applications particularly in Industries like mining urban planning and civil engineering where accurate terrain models are crucial these models Aid in planning excavation Road layouts and Hazard mitigation there are two primary methods of creating these Maps photogrammetry which generates 3D models using overlapping aerial images that are run through a specialized software and lidar a more accurate but expensive method the value of the data in this field allows for premium pricing making drone 3D modeling a highly profitable Venture with significant demand number three inspections drones offer a safer and faster solution for inspecting miles and miles of power lines pipelines Construction sites cell towers rooftops and more equipped with thermal cameras drones can identify things like power line damage by detecting heat variations caused by disrupted current flow replacing weeks of work with just a few hours this technology also proves valuable for drone rooftop inspections benefiting real estate agents roof repair teams solar installers insurance adjusters and homeowners the appearance and condition of roofs significantly impact property values which can drive high demand for drone based assessments these inspections eliminate hazards logistical challenges and the need for repeat surveys number four search and rescue globally First Responders have integrated drones to enhance their capabilities particularly in search and rescue missions drones excel in covering large areas swiftly offering aerial overviews and Rapid deployment equipped with thermal cameras these drones can detect anomalies even in darkness or obscured environments some models even feature things like loudspeakers and spotlights to help find lost individuals the cost-effective nature of drones versus using helicopters makes them an incredibly attractive option law enforcement agencies benefit from having in-house Pilots for immediate response but offering Training Services to departments that are still seeking drone pilot expertise remains a valuable Avenue number five education and consultation maybe you can't decide on any particular industry but still want to be involved with flying drones and sharing your expertise and passion with other people becoming a drone flight instructor is an appealing option for those who enjoy teaching and communication unlike manned Airport flight instruction there are currently no official FAA certifications for drone flight instruction instead various organizations establish their own criteria another way to educate would be through consultation implementing drone programs can be really complex and a drone consulting firm can help launch a successful drone program items by providing training guidance and legal compliance these Services can also ensure adherence to evolving laws especially the faa's part 107 investing in a drone Consulting service is invaluable to avoid costly mistakes and ensure a successful drone program with a quick return on investment these are just five   2. @financial.crimes_aggie [7 likes]: Drones and drone pilots are here to stay. But to be frank, I belive it will get much harder for individuals who are just drone piltos to find meaningful, decent-paying work. One example is the comment about law enforcement. A couple of years ago, yes, law enforcement were enlisting private companies to teach drone piloting. However, now there is the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center (FLETC) that is training police officers in such remote piloting. My uncle is a GIS expert, and drones are pretty important, but it is expected that a GIS Technician, Specialist is familiar with operating a drone. The same could be said for meteorology, surveying, and engineering. Yes, it was acceptable to just be a drone pilot in years past; however, companies are now expecting their engineers, their GIS specialists, cartographers, meteorologists, et cetera to already have drone piloting as an add-on skill. Therefore, if you want to be a drone pilot, learn another skill that's adjacent to drones as your primary skill/industry, then just pick up remote piloting as a bonus.
